subject: Mathematics
q: The total number of three-digit numbers, with one digit repeated exactly two times, is ______.
question: The total number of three-digit numbers, with one digit repeated exactly two times, is ______.
solution:
For three digit number we have following cases,

Case I: When digit zero is repeated

Number of numbers =C19=9

Case II: When digit zero is present but not repeated

Number of numbers =C19·2!=18

Case III: When digit zero is not present

Number of numbers =9·8·3!2!=216

Total number of numbers =243

subject: Mathematics
q: There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> men and two women participating in a chess tournament. Each participant plays two games with every other participant. If the number of games played by the men between themselves exceeds the number of games played between the men and the women by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is :
question: There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> men and two women participating in a chess tournament. Each participant plays two games with every other participant. If the number of games played by the men between themselves exceeds the number of games played between the men and the women by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is :
solution:
Given, number of men is m and number of women is 2.

Since each participants competes two games with each other, Hence count of men Vs men total number of game plays =2 mC2.

count of men Vs women total number of game plays =4m.

As per given condition in question,

2 mC2=84+4 m

⇒m2-5 m-84=0

⇒m2-12 m+7 m-84=0

⇒m-12m+7=0.

⇒m=12, -7

subject: Mathematics
q: The letters of the word "UDAYPUR" are written in all possible ways with or without meaning and these words are arranged as in a dictionary. The rank of the word "UDAYPUR" is
question: The letters of the word "UDAYPUR" are written in all possible ways with or without meaning and these words are arranged as in a dictionary. The rank of the word "UDAYPUR" is
solution:
Letters of UDAYPUR: A, D, P, R, U, U, Y (U repeated twice).
Words starting with a letter before U: letters A, D, P, R each give $\frac{6!}{2!} = 360$ arrangements $\Rightarrow 4 \times 360 = 1440$.
Fix U at position 1. Remaining: A, D, P, R, U, Y (all distinct).
Words starting with UA: $5! = 120$.
Fix UD at positions 1-2. Remaining: A, P, R, U, Y. No letter before A, so fix UDA.
Remaining: P, R, U, Y. Letters before Y: P, R, U give $3 \times 3! = 18$.
Fix UDAY. Remaining: P, R, U. No letter before P, fix UDAYP.
Remaining: R, U. Letter before U: R gives $1! = 1$.
Fix UDAYPU, last letter R: the word itself.
Rank $= 1440 + 120 + 18 + 1 + 1 = 1580$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of four letter words that can be formed using the letters of the word BARRACK is
question: The number of four letter words that can be formed using the letters of the word BARRACK is
solution:
If all four letters are different then the number of words ${ }^5 \mathrm{C}_4 \times 4 !=120$
If two letters are $\mathrm{R}$ and other two different letters are chosen from $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}$ then the number of words $={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \frac{4 !}{2 !}=72$
If two letters are $\mathrm{A}$ and other two different letters are chosen from $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}$ then the number of words $={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \frac{4 !}{2 !}=72$
If word is formed using two $R$ 's and two $A$ 's then the number of words $=\frac{4 !}{2 ! 2 !}=6$
Therefore, the number of four-letter words that can be formed $=120+72+72+6=270$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A=\{(a,b,c): a,b,c \text{ are non-negative integers and } a+b+2c=22\}$. Then $n(A)$ is equal to:
question: Let $A=\{(a,b,c): a,b,c \text{ are non-negative integers and } a+b+2c=22\}$. Then $n(A)$ is equal to:
solution:
The given equation is $a + b + 2c = 22$, where $a, b, c$ are non-negative integers.

This can be rewritten as $a + b = 22 - 2c$.

Since $a \ge 0$ and $b \ge 0$, we must have $22 - 2c \ge 0$, which gives $c \le 11$.

Since $c$ is a non-negative integer, the possible values for $c$ are $0, 1, 2, \dots, 11$.

For a fixed value of $c$, the number of non-negative integer solutions to the equation $a + b = 22 - 2c$ is given by $(22 - 2c) + 1 = 23 - 2c$.

The total number of solutions $n(A)$ is the sum of the number of solutions for each possible value of $c$:

$n(A) = \sum_{c=0}^{11} (23 - 2c)$

$n(A) = 23 + 21 + 19 + \dots + 1$

This is an arithmetic progression with $12$ terms, first term $1$, and last term $23$.

$n(A) = \dfrac{12}{2} (1 + 23) = 6 \times 24 = 144$

Answer: $144$

q: The number of ways of selecting <math><mn>15</mn></math> teams from <math><mn>15</mn></math> men and <math><mn>15</mn></math> women, such that each team consists of a man and a woman is
question: The number of ways of selecting <math><mn>15</mn></math> teams from <math><mn>15</mn></math> men and <math><mn>15</mn></math> women, such that each team consists of a man and a woman is
solution: First team can be selected in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mo>(</mo><mn>15</mn></msup><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msup><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn></msup><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> way, second in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mo>(</mo><mn>14</mn></msup><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msup><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></msup><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> way and so on. So, number of ways of selecting <math><mn>15</mn></math> teams <math><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>15</mn><mo>!</mo></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>15</mn><mo>!</mo></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>15</mn><mo>!</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> be a non-negative integer. Then the number of divisors of the form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> of the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mn>10</mn></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>11</mn></mfenced><mn>11</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>13</mn></mfenced><mn>13</mn></msup></math> is equal to _____.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> be a non-negative integer. Then the number of divisors of the form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> of the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mn>10</mn></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>11</mn></mfenced><mn>11</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>13</mn></mfenced><mn>13</mn></msup></math> is equal to _____.
solution:
Let N=210×510×1111×1313

Now, power of 2 must be zero,

Power of 5 can be anything,

Power of 13 can be anything.

But, power of 11 should be even.

So, required number of divisors is

1×11×14×6=924.

subject: Mathematics
q: How many different words can be formed by jumbling the letters in the word MISSISSIPPI in which no two $\mathrm{S}$ are adjacent?
question: How many different words can be formed by jumbling the letters in the word MISSISSIPPI in which no two $\mathrm{S}$ are adjacent?
solution:
Other than $\mathrm{S}$, seven letters $\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{I}, \mathrm{I}, \mathrm{I}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{I}$ can be arranged in $\frac{7 !}{2 ! 4 !}=7.5 .3$.
Now four $\mathrm{S}$ can be placed in 8 spaces in ${ }^8 \mathrm{C}_4$ ways.
Desired number of ways $=7.5 \cdot 3 \cdot{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_4=7 \cdot{ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4 \cdot{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_4$

q: If all the six digit numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> are arranged in the increasing order, then the sum of the digits in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>72</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> number is _______.
question: If all the six digit numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> are arranged in the increasing order, then the sum of the digits in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>72</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> number is _______.
solution:
Taking case 1 fixing 1 & 2 at first two places,

1 2        

So, other number can be selected in C47=35 ways as number are from 1-9

Case 2 fixing 1 & 3 at first two place,

1 3        

So, other number can be selected inC46=15 ways

Similarly,

1 4        

So, other number can be selected inC45=5 ways

Now fixing 1 & 5 we get,

1 5        

Other number can be selected inC44=1

Now fixing 2 & 3 in first two place,

2 3        

So, other number can be selected in C46=15

So, the 72nd number will be 245678

Hence, sum will be 2+4+5+6+7+8=32

q: The sum of all the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit distinct numbers that can be formed with the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is:
question: The sum of all the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit distinct numbers that can be formed with the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is:
solution:
Digits are 1, 2, 2, 3

total distinct numbers 4!2!=12.

total numbers when

1 at unit place is 3.
2 at unit place is 6
3 at unit place is 3.

So, sum =(3+12+9)103+102+10+1

=(1111)×24

=26664

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of ways, 5 boys and 4 girls can sit in a row so that either all the boys sit together or no two boys sit together, is $\qquad$ -
question: The number of ways, 5 boys and 4 girls can sit in a row so that either all the boys sit together or no two boys sit together, is $\qquad$ -
solution:
A : number of ways that all boys sit together $=5!\times 5!$
B : number of ways if no 2 boys
sit together $=4!\times 5$ !
$\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B}=\phi$
Required no. of ways $=5!\times 5!+4!\times 5!=17280$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of numbers greater than 5000, less than 9000 and divisible by 3, that can be formed using the digits $0,1,2,5,9$, if the repetition of the digits is allowed, is $\_\_\_\_$
question: The number of numbers greater than 5000, less than 9000 and divisible by 3, that can be formed using the digits $0,1,2,5,9$, if the repetition of the digits is allowed, is $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
(1) all different
$5, 0, 1, 9 \Rightarrow \lfloor 3 = 6$ ways
(2) 2 alike, 2 different
$5, 0, 0, 1 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5, 1, 1, 2 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5, 2, 2, 0 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5, 2, 2, 9 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5, 5, 0, 2 \Rightarrow 6$ ways
$5, 5, 2, 9 \Rightarrow 6$ ways
$5, 1, 9, 9 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
(3) 3 alike, 1 different
$5, 5, 5, 0 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5, 5, 5, 9 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
(4) 2 alike, 2 other alike
$5, 5, 1, 1 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
Total ways $= 42$

subject: Mathematics
q: All the letters of the word PUBLIC are written in all possible orders and these words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. Then the serial number of the word PUBLIC is
question: All the letters of the word PUBLIC are written in all possible orders and these words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. Then the serial number of the word PUBLIC is
solution:
The given word is PUBLIC

Arranging the letters alphabetically, we get

BCILPU

When the word starts with any of the letters B/C/I/L, the number of possibilities=5!×4=480

Now when the word starts with PB, then the number of possibilities=4!=24

Now when the word starts with PC, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with PI, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with PL, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with PUBC, then the number of possibilities=2!=2

Now when the word starts with PUBI, then the number of possibilities =2!=2

Now when the word starts with PUBLC, then the number of possibilities=1

Now when the word starts with PUBLIC, then the number of possibilities =1

Rank =480+24×4+2×2+1×2=582

Hence, rank of the work PUBLIC is 582.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let S denote the set of 4-digit numbers $a b c d$ such that $a>b>c>d$ and P denote the set of 5 -digit numbers having product of its digits equal to 20. Then $n(\mathrm{~S})+n(\mathrm{P})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
question: Let S denote the set of 4-digit numbers $a b c d$ such that $a>b>c>d$ and P denote the set of 5 -digit numbers having product of its digits equal to 20. Then $n(\mathrm{~S})+n(\mathrm{P})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
$n(S)$: Choose 4 distinct digits from $\{0,1,\ldots,9\}$ and arrange in decreasing order. $n(S) = \binom{10}{4} = 210$.
$n(P)$: 5-digit numbers with digit product $= 20 = 2^2 \times 5$. All digits $\ge 1$ (else product $= 0$). Must include exactly one $5$, remaining 4 digits multiply to $4$.
Case 1: $\{1,1,1,4,5\}$: $\frac{5!}{3!} = 20$ arrangements.
Case 2: $\{1,1,2,2,5\}$: $\frac{5!}{2!\cdot 2!} = 30$ arrangements.
$n(P) = 50$. Hence $n(S) + n(P) = 210 + 50 = 260$.

subject: Mathematics
q: In an examination of Mathematics paper, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> questions of equal marks and the question paper is divided into three sections : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. A student is required to attempt total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> questions taking at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> questions from each section. If section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> questions, section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> questions and section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> questions, then the total number of ways a student can select <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> questions is _________.
question: In an examination of Mathematics paper, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> questions of equal marks and the question paper is divided into three sections : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. A student is required to attempt total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> questions taking at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> questions from each section. If section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> questions, section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> questions and section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> questions, then the total number of ways a student can select <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> questions is _________.
solution:
Given,

Mathematics paper has 20 questions of equal marks

and have three sections A, B & C, also A has 8 questions, B has 6 questions and C has 6 questions,

Now, if 4 questions from each section are selected,

then remaining 3 questions can be selected either in (1, 1,1) or (3,0,0) or (2,1,0) {as we have already selected 4×3=12 questions}

So, the total ways will be=C58·C56·C56+C68C56·C46×2+C58·C66·C46×2+C48·C66·C56×2+C78·C46·C46

=56·6·6+28·6·15·2+56·15·2+70·6·2+8·15·15

=2016+5040+1680+840+1800=11376

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> digit numbers, that can be formed using all the digits of the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>123412341</mn></math> so that the even digits occupy only even places, is ______
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> digit numbers, that can be formed using all the digits of the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>123412341</mn></math> so that the even digits occupy only even places, is ______
solution:
According to the question,

1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4

there are four even digits as 2 and 4 are repeating two times each. 
So, the even digits will occupy the even places in 4!(2! 2!) = 6 ways

3 and 1 are repeating two and three times respectively.
So, the remaining places will be occupied by odd digits in 5!(3! 2!)=10 ways

Therefore, total 9 digits that can be formed = 6×10 = 60.
Hence, the answer is 60.

subject: Mathematics
q: Line $L_1$ of slope 2 and line $L_2$ of slope $\frac{1}{2}$ intersect at the origin O . In the first quadrant, $\mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \ldots . \mathrm{P}_{12}$ are 12 points on line $L_1$ and $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots . . Q_9$ are 9 points on line $L_2$. Then the total number of triangles, that can be formed having vertices at three of the 22 points $\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \ldots \mathrm{P}_{12}$, $\mathrm{Q}_1, \mathrm{Q}_2, \ldots . \mathrm{Q}_9$, is:
question: Line $L_1$ of slope 2 and line $L_2$ of slope $\frac{1}{2}$ intersect at the origin O . In the first quadrant, $\mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \ldots . \mathrm{P}_{12}$ are 12 points on line $L_1$ and $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots . . Q_9$ are 9 points on line $L_2$. Then the total number of triangles, that can be formed having vertices at three of the 22 points $\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \ldots \mathrm{P}_{12}$, $\mathrm{Q}_1, \mathrm{Q}_2, \ldots . \mathrm{Q}_9$, is:
solution:
Total number of $\Delta$ are
$\begin{aligned}
& ={ }^9 \mathrm{C}_1{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_2{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^1 \mathrm{C}_1{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_1{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_1 \\ & =594+432+108 \\ & =1134
\end{aligned}$

question: Group A consists of 7 boys and 3 girls, while group B consists of 6 boys and 5 girls. The number of ways, 4 boys and 4 girls can be invited for a picnic if 5 of them must be from group $A$ and the remaining 3 from group $B$, is equal to :

subject: Mathematics
q: A man <math><mi>X</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> friends, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> of them are ladies and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> are men. His wife <math><mi>Y</mi></math> also has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> friends, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> of them are ladies and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> are men. Assume <math><mi>X</mi></math> and <math><mi>Y</mi></math> have no common friends. Then the total number of ways in which <math><mi>X</mi></math> and <math><mi>Y</mi></math> together can throw a party inviting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> ladies and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> men, so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> friends of each of <math><mi>X</mi></math> and <math><mi>Y</mi></math> are in this party is:
question: A man <math><mi>X</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> friends, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> of them are ladies and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> are men. Assume <math><mi>X</mi></math> and <math><mi>Y</mi></math> have no common friends. Then the total number of ways in which <math><mi>X</mi></math> and <math><mi>Y</mi></math> together can throw a party inviting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> ladies and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> men, so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> friends of each of <math><mi>X</mi></math> and <math><mi>Y</mi></math> are in this party is:
solution:
Here, m - men and L - ladies



Total=9×16+1+9×36+16

=144+1+324+16

=485

subject: Mathematics
q: A person forgets his <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit ATM pin code. But he remembers that in the code all the digits are different, the greatest digit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> and the sum of the first two digits is equal to the sum of the last two digits. Then the maximum number of trials necessary to obtain the correct code is________.
question: A person forgets his <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit ATM pin code. But he remembers that in the code all the digits are different, the greatest digit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> and the sum of the first two digits is equal to the sum of the last two digits. Then the maximum number of trials necessary to obtain the correct code is________.
solution:
Let the 4-digit pin be abcd

Now given sum of first two is equal to last two, so a+b=c+d

And number available are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

Now Let a+b=c+d=λ

Also the greatest digit that he has used is 7

So, a+b=c+d=λ≥7

Now taking all cases starting from λ=7 we get,

λ=7, a,b→0,7 or 7,0, then c,d→1,6,2,5,3,4,4,3,5,2,6,1 and vice-versa, so 2×12=24 numbers

λ=8, a,b→1,7 or 7,1, then c,d→2,6,3,5,6,2,5,3 and vice versa so total 2×8=16 numbers,

λ=9, a,b→2,7 or 7,2 then c,d→3,6,4,5,5,4,6,3 and vice versa, so total 2×8=16 numbers,

λ=10, a, b→3, 7,7, 3, so c, d→6, 4,4, 6→8 numbers

λ=11, a, b→4, 7,7, 4, so c, d→6, 5,5, 6→8 numbers

λ=12, 13 & 14 are not possible,

So, Total numbers =24+16+16+8+8=72

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of six letter words (with or without meaning), formed using all the letters of the word 'VOWELS', so that all the consonants never come together, is
question: The number of six letter words (with or without meaning), formed using all the letters of the word 'VOWELS', so that all the consonants never come together, is
solution:
Given word 'VOWELS'

Number of words Arranging all the letters = 6!

In the given word Consonants are W, L, S.

Treating all the consonants as one unit then number of units will be 4.

No of such arrangements =4!.3!

VOWELS all the consonants never come together =Total - all consonants come together =6!-4!×3!=576

q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-letter words, with or without meaning, each consisting of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> vowels and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> consonants, which can be formed from the letters of the word UNIVERSE without repetition is _____.
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-letter words, with or without meaning, each consisting of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> vowels and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> consonants, which can be formed from the letters of the word UNIVERSE without repetition is _____.
solution:
Given,

We have to make four letter word from UNIVERSE each consisting 2 vowels and 2 consonants,

Now in UNIVERSE, we have,

E, E, I, U, (Vowels) + N, R, S, V (Consonants)

2 different vowels + 2 consonants can be arrange in 3C2·4C2·4!=3×6×24=432 ways

{C23 is taken because of repetition of letter E }

solution:

And, x,y are distinct integers.

Let

x+y=5k, where k∈N

So, we have

x y   Number of ways
5λ i.e, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 5λ i.e, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 Since, x and y are distinct integers, so we cannot pair 5,5, 10,10,...25,25 20
 5λ+1 i.e., 1, 6, 11, 16, 21  5λ+4 i.e., 4, 9, 14, 19, 14   25
 5λ+2 i.e, 2,7, 12, 17, 22  5λ+3 i.e., 3, 8, 13, 18, 23   25
 5λ+3 i.e., 3, 8, 13, 18, 23 5λ+2 i.e, 2, 7, 12, 17, 22   25
 5λ+4 i.e., 4, 9, 14, 19, 14 5λ+1 i.e, 1, 6, 11, 16, 21   25

Total number of ways =120

The number of such numbers in which the odd digits occupy even places is
question: All possible numbers are formed using the digits <math><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>4</mn><mi> </mi></math> taken all at a time. The number of such numbers in which the odd digits occupy even places is
solution:
Odd digits: 1, 1, 3
Even digits: 2, 2, 2, 2, 4, 4
The number of ways of placing odd digits at even position = C34×3!2!=4×3=12
The number of ways of placing even digits =6!4! 2!=15 
∴ Total number of ways =12×15=180

q: A committee of 4 persons is to be formed from 2 ladies, 2 old men and 4 young men such that it includes at least 1 lady, at least 1 old man and at most 2 young men. Then the total number of ways in which this committee can be formed is :
question: A committee of 4 persons is to be formed from 2 ladies, 2 old men and 4 young men such that it includes at least 1 lady, at least 1 old man and at most 2 young men. Then the total number of ways in which this committee can be formed is :
solution: Required number of ways $$ \begin{aligned} & ={ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \\ & { }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_0 \\ & =2 \times 2 \times \frac{4 \times 3}{2} \\ & \quad \quad \quad 2 \times 1 \times 4+1 \times 2 \times 4+1 \times 1 \times 1 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of $4$-letter words, with or without meaning, each consisting of two vowels and two consonants that can be formed from the letters of the word INCONSEQUENTIAL, without repeating any letter, is:
question: The number of $4$-letter words, with or without meaning, each consisting of two vowels and two consonants that can be formed from the letters of the word INCONSEQUENTIAL, without repeating any letter, is:
solution:
The given word is INCONSEQUENTIAL.

The vowels present in the word are I, O, E, U, A. There are $5$ distinct vowels.

The consonants present in the word are N, C, S, Q, T, L. There are $6$ distinct consonants.

We need to form $4$-letter words consisting of $2$ vowels and $2$ consonants without repeating any letter. This means all $4$ letters in the formed word must be distinct.

Number of ways to select $2$ distinct vowels from $5$ distinct vowels = $^{5}C_{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$

Number of ways to select $2$ distinct consonants from $6$ distinct consonants = $^{6}C_{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$

Number of ways to arrange the $4$ selected letters = $4! = 24$

Total number of $4$-letter words that can be formed = $10 \times 15 \times 24 = 3600$

Answer: $3600$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of ways, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> identical cubes, of which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> are blue and rest are red, can be placed in a row so that between any two red cubes there should be at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> blue cubes, is ______.
question: The number of ways, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> identical cubes, of which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> are blue and rest are red, can be placed in a row so that between any two red cubes there should be at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> blue cubes, is ______.
solution:
Given, 4 blue & 5 red spherical ball

Now according to question there should be minimum 2 blue ball in between any two red ball.

So assume a,b,c,d,e,f are number of blue balls,

So, a+b+c+d+e+f=11  ......(i)

Where b,c,d,e⩾2

Now let b=b'+2

c=c'+2

d=d'+2

e=e'+2

So equation (i) becomes

a+b'+2+c'+2+d'+2+e'+2+f=11

⇒ a+b'+c'+d'+e'+f=3

Now total ways will be

= 3+6-1C6-1=8C5=8C3=56

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of triplets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are distinct non negative integers satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn></math>, is
question: The number of triplets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are distinct non negative integers satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn></math>, is
solution:
Given,

x+y+z=15

Now we know that,

Non-negative integral solution of equation a+b+c=n is given by C3-1n+3-1 where a=b=c & a=b≠c are also possibilities

So by above formula we get,

Total number of non-negative solution will be,  C3-115+3-1=C217

Now solving If any of these 2 are equal
So, the equation will become x+2y=15

Now finding possible cases we get,

y=0x=15

y=1x=13

y=2x=11

y=3x=9

y=4x=7

y=5  x=5→x=y=z=5

y=6  x=3

y=7  x=1

So, total possibilities where x, y & z are distinct will be,

=C217-C23×8+2

{Note adding 2 because the cases x=y=z is subtracted three times}

=136-24+2=114

q: Let<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>></mo><mn>2</mn></math> be an integer. Suppose that there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> Metro stations in a city located around a circular path. Each pair of the nearest stations is connected by a straight track only. Further, each pair of the nearest station is connected by blue line, whereas all remaining pairs of stations are connected by red line. If number of red lines is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math> times the number of blue lines, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is
question: Let<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>></mo><mn>2</mn></math> be an integer. Suppose that there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> Metro stations in a city located around a circular path. Each pair of the nearest stations is connected by a straight track only. Further, each pair of the nearest station is connected by blue line, whereas all remaining pairs of stations are connected by red line. If number of red lines is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math> times the number of blue lines, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is
solution:
Two consecutive stations =n 

Two non-consecutive stations=C2n-n

 C2n-n=99n

⇒nn-12-n=99n

⇒n2-n2=100n

⇒n2=201n

⇒n=0, 201 

n=0 is not possible.

⇒n=201

subject: Mathematics
q: How many ways are there to arrange the letters in the word GARDEN with the vowels in alphabetical order?
question: How many ways are there to arrange the letters in the word GARDEN with the vowels in alphabetical order?
solution: Since in half the arrangement $A$ will be before $E$ and other half $E$ will be before $A$. Hence total number of ways $=\frac{6 !}{2}=360$.

subject: Mathematics
q: A person has three different bags and four different books. The number of ways, in which he can put these books in the bags so that no bag is empty, is:
question: A person has three different bags and four different books. The number of ways, in which he can put these books in the bags so that no bag is empty, is:
solution:
To ensure no bag is empty, exactly one bag must contain 2 books and the other two bags must contain 1 book each.

The number of ways to choose 2 books out of 4 to be kept together is $^{4}C_{2} = 6$

Now, we have 3 units (one pair of books and two single books) to be distributed into the 3 different bags.

The number of ways to distribute these 3 units into 3 bags is $3! = 6$

Total number of ways = $6 \times 6 = 36$

Answer: $36$

subject: Mathematics
q: If all the words (with or without meaning) having five letters, formed using the letters of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mi>M</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>L</mi><mi>L</mi></math> and arranged as in a dictionary; then the position of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mi>M</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>L</mi><mi>L</mi></math> is
question: If all the words (with or without meaning) having five letters, formed using the letters of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mi>M</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>L</mi><mi>L</mi></math> and arranged as in a dictionary; then the position of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mi>M</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>L</mi><mi>L</mi></math> is
solution:
SMALL
Total number of words formed would be 5!2!=60
When arranged as per dictionary. The words starting from A

A- - - -4!2!=12

The words starting from L

L- - - - = 4!=24

The words starting from M

M- - - - =4!2!=12

The words starting from S

S A- - - =3!2!=3

S L- - - 3!=6
S M A L L =1
∴ Rank would be 12 + 24 + 12 + 3 + 6 + 1 = 58
